scenarioDescription: A multinational corporation needs to connect their on-premises data center to AWS with a dedicated, private 10 Gbps connection. They require consistent low-latency performance for a mission-critical SAP application and need a backup path that fails over automatically if the primary connection goes down.
toolboxServices: AWS Direct Connect|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|AWS Transit Gateway|Amazon VPC|AWS Global Accelerator|AWS PrivateLink|Amazon CloudFront
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Direct Connect|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|AWS Transit Gateway
hint: The primary connection requires a dedicated physical circuit, not an internet-based tunnel.
hint2: The backup path uses an encrypted tunnel over the public internet as a cost-effective failover option.
explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides the dedicated 10 Gbps private connection with consistent low latency for the SAP workload. AWS Site-to-Site VPN serves as the backup failover path over the public internet, providing encrypted connectivity if the Direct Connect link fails. AWS Transit Gateway acts as the central hub that routes traffic between the on-premises network and multiple VPCs, enabling automatic failover between the Direct Connect and VPN paths using BGP route propagation.

scenarioDescription: A SaaS company needs to expose their internal REST API to hundreds of enterprise customers without traversing the public internet. Each customer has their own AWS account and VPC, and the SaaS provider wants to maintain control over which customers can access the service.
toolboxServices: AWS PrivateLink|VPC Peering|AWS Transit Gateway|Network Load Balancer|Application Load Balancer|Amazon API Gateway|AWS Direct Connect
correctPipelineOrder: Network Load Balancer|AWS PrivateLink
hint: The service provider must front their application with a specific type of load balancer before creating the endpoint service.
hint2: This AWS service creates private connectivity between VPCs without requiring peering, VPN, or Direct Connect.
explanation: A Network Load Balancer is required as the front end for the SaaS application because AWS PrivateLink endpoint services only support NLB (not ALB). AWS PrivateLink then creates VPC endpoint services that customers connect to via interface VPC endpoints in their own accounts, keeping all traffic on the AWS private network without traversing the public internet. The provider controls access by managing endpoint connection acceptance and permissions policies.

scenarioDescription: A gaming company needs to route players to the nearest AWS Region for their multiplayer servers. Players connect over UDP, and the company needs to minimize jitter and packet loss while providing static IP addresses that can be allowlisted by corporate firewalls.
toolboxServices: AWS Global Accelerator|Amazon CloudFront|Amazon Route 53|Network Load Balancer|Application Load Balancer|Elastic Load Balancing|AWS WAF
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Global Accelerator|Network Load Balancer
hint: This service provides two static anycast IP addresses and routes traffic over the AWS global network backbone.
hint2: The gaming servers use UDP, so the load balancer must support Layer 4 protocols, not just HTTP/HTTPS.
explanation: AWS Global Accelerator provides two static anycast IP addresses and routes player traffic onto the AWS global network at the nearest edge location, minimizing jitter and packet loss by avoiding unpredictable public internet routing. Network Load Balancer is the correct backend target because it supports UDP traffic at Layer 4, which is essential for multiplayer gaming protocols. CloudFront would be wrong here because it only supports HTTP/HTTPS and does not handle UDP traffic.

scenarioDescription: A financial services company has 200 VPCs across 4 AWS Regions that all need to communicate with each other and with 3 on-premises data centers. They need centralized routing, network segmentation between production and development environments, and the ability to inspect all inter-VPC traffic with a third-party firewall appliance.
toolboxServices: AWS Transit Gateway|VPC Peering|AWS Direct Connect Gateway|AWS Network Firewall|Transit Gateway Inter-Region Peering|AWS PrivateLink|Amazon VPC
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Direct Connect Gateway|AWS Transit Gateway|Transit Gateway Inter-Region Peering|AWS Network Firewall
hint: On-premises data centers connect to AWS through a gateway that associates with Transit Gateways across multiple Regions.
hint2: Transit Gateway route tables enable network segmentation, and inter-Region peering extends connectivity globally.
explanation: AWS Direct Connect Gateway connects the 3 on-premises data centers to Transit Gateways across all 4 Regions. AWS Transit Gateway in each Region acts as the central hub for all VPCs, using separate route tables to segment production and development traffic. Transit Gateway Inter-Region Peering connects the Transit Gateways across Regions, and AWS Network Firewall inspects all inter-VPC traffic by routing it through a centralized inspection VPC.

scenarioDescription: A media company needs to deliver live video streaming content to millions of global viewers with ultra-low latency. They also need to protect the origin servers from DDoS attacks and ensure only authenticated users can access premium content streams.
toolboxServices: Amazon CloudFront|AWS Shield Advanced|AWS WAF|Amazon S3|AWS Global Accelerator|Amazon Route 53|Elastic Load Balancing
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Route 53|Amazon CloudFront|AWS WAF|AWS Shield Advanced
hint: DNS resolution is always the first step when a viewer types in the streaming URL.
hint2: The CDN service supports real-time streaming and can be paired with signed URLs for authenticated access.
explanation: Amazon Route 53 resolves the streaming domain to CloudFront edge locations using latency-based routing. Amazon CloudFront delivers the live video streams from edge locations closest to viewers, supporting real-time streaming protocols and signed URLs/cookies for premium content authentication. AWS WAF filters malicious requests at the edge with custom rules, and AWS Shield Advanced provides managed DDoS protection with 24/7 DDoS Response Team support for the entire delivery pipeline.

scenarioDescription: A healthcare organization needs to resolve DNS queries for their private hosted zone from on-premises servers. The on-premises DNS infrastructure must be able to forward queries for AWS-hosted domains to Route 53, and AWS resources must also resolve on-premises domain names.
toolboxServices: Amazon Route 53 Resolver|Route 53 Resolver Inbound Endpoint|Route 53 Resolver Outbound Endpoint|Amazon Route 53|AWS Direct Connect|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|AWS Transit Gateway
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Direct Connect|Route 53 Resolver Inbound Endpoint|Route 53 Resolver Outbound Endpoint
hint: A private network connection must exist between on-premises and AWS before any DNS resolution can occur.
hint2: Inbound endpoints receive DNS queries FROM on-premises; outbound endpoints send DNS queries TO on-premises.
explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides the private network path between on-premises and AWS, which is required for DNS traffic to flow. Route 53 Resolver Inbound Endpoint receives DNS queries from on-premises DNS servers and resolves them against Route 53 private hosted zones. Route 53 Resolver Outbound Endpoint forwards DNS queries from AWS resources to on-premises DNS servers using conditional forwarding rules, completing the bidirectional hybrid DNS architecture.

scenarioDescription: A retail company wants to migrate from a traditional three-tier web architecture to a microservices model. Each microservice runs in its own VPC and needs to communicate privately. They need service discovery so microservices can find each other by name without hardcoding IP addresses.
toolboxServices: AWS Cloud Map|Amazon Route 53|VPC Peering|AWS PrivateLink|AWS App Mesh|Amazon ECS|AWS Transit Gateway
correctPipelineOrder: VPC Peering|AWS Cloud Map|AWS App Mesh
hint: Before services can discover each other, the VPCs they live in must have network-level connectivity.
hint2: A managed service registry allows microservices to register and discover each other using DNS or API-based lookups.
explanation: VPC Peering establishes private network connectivity between the microservice VPCs so traffic can flow between them. AWS Cloud Map provides service discovery, allowing each microservice to register itself and be discovered by name through DNS or API queries without hardcoding IP addresses. AWS App Mesh provides the service mesh layer that manages traffic routing, observability, and resilience between microservices using Envoy proxies.

scenarioDescription: A government agency requires that all traffic between their VPC subnets be inspected by a stateful firewall for compliance. They need to log all accepted and rejected traffic flows, detect intrusion attempts, and automatically block traffic matching known threat signatures.
toolboxServices: AWS Network Firewall|Security Groups|Network ACLs|VPC Flow Logs|Amazon GuardDuty|AWS WAF|Amazon Inspector
correctPipelineOrder: VPC Flow Logs|AWS Network Firewall|Amazon GuardDuty
hint: First, enable comprehensive logging of all network traffic metadata across all subnets.
hint2: The managed firewall service supports stateful inspection, IDS/IPS, and Suricata-compatible rule groups.
explanation: VPC Flow Logs capture metadata for all accepted and rejected traffic flows across subnets, providing the compliance audit trail. AWS Network Firewall performs stateful deep packet inspection between subnets using Suricata-compatible rules for intrusion detection/prevention and automatically blocks traffic matching known threat signatures. Amazon GuardDuty continuously analyzes VPC Flow Logs and other data sources using ML to detect anomalous network behavior and potential threats that rule-based inspection might miss.

scenarioDescription: A company is deploying a multi-account AWS organization with 50 accounts. They need all accounts to share a common set of subnets for cost efficiency, while maintaining account-level isolation for security. Each account should be able to launch resources into shared subnets without managing their own VPC infrastructure.
toolboxServices: AWS Resource Access Manager|AWS Transit Gateway|VPC Peering|AWS Organizations|Amazon VPC|AWS PrivateLink|AWS Control Tower
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Organizations|Amazon VPC|AWS Resource Access Manager
hint: Multi-account governance must be established before any resource sharing can occur.
hint2: A specific AWS service allows you to share VPC subnets across accounts within an Organization.
explanation: AWS Organizations provides the multi-account structure and organizational units needed for governance. Amazon VPC is created in a central networking account with the shared subnets designed for cross-account use. AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) shares those VPC subnets with participant accounts across the Organization, allowing each account to launch resources into shared subnets while maintaining account-level isolation — this is the AWS VPC Sharing pattern.

scenarioDescription: An e-commerce company needs to implement IPv6 for their public-facing web application while maintaining backward compatibility with IPv4 clients. Internal backend services should remain IPv4-only, and the architecture must handle the translation between IPv6 external traffic and IPv4 internal traffic.
toolboxServices: Application Load Balancer|Network Load Balancer|NAT Gateway|Elastic IP|Amazon CloudFront|Amazon VPC (dual-stack)|AWS Global Accelerator
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon CloudFront|Application Load Balancer|NAT Gateway
hint: The CDN edge layer can accept both IPv4 and IPv6 connections from clients natively.
hint2: The load balancer in dual-stack mode translates IPv6 traffic to IPv4 for backend targets.
explanation: Amazon CloudFront natively supports dual-stack (IPv4 and IPv6), accepting connections from all client types at the edge. The Application Load Balancer operates in dual-stack mode, receiving IPv6 traffic from CloudFront and translating it to IPv4 when forwarding to backend targets that remain IPv4-only. NAT Gateway provides outbound internet access for the private IPv4 backend services that need to reach external APIs or download updates.

scenarioDescription: A bank needs to establish a highly available Direct Connect architecture that can survive the failure of any single Direct Connect location, any single connection, or any single device. The architecture must meet the AWS maximum resiliency recommendation and connect to a single AWS Region.
toolboxServices: AWS Direct Connect|AWS Direct Connect Gateway|Virtual Private Gateway|AWS Transit Gateway|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|Link Aggregation Group (LAG)|Amazon VPC
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Direct Connect|AWS Direct Connect|Virtual Private Gateway
hint: Maximum resiliency requires connections at TWO separate Direct Connect locations, not just one.
hint2: Each Direct Connect location should have at least two independent connections for device-level redundancy.
explanation: AWS Direct Connect connections are provisioned at two separate Direct Connect locations (minimum 4 total connections — 2 per location) to survive the failure of any single location, connection, or device. This is the AWS maximum resiliency model. A Virtual Private Gateway is attached to the VPC and terminates the private virtual interfaces from all Direct Connect connections, using BGP to automatically failover between paths when any component fails.

scenarioDescription: A company wants to centralize outbound internet access for 30 VPCs through a single inspection point. All outbound traffic must be filtered by domain name, and only traffic to approved domains should be allowed. They also need to log all DNS queries for security auditing.
toolboxServices: AWS Network Firewall|NAT Gateway|AWS Transit Gateway|Route 53 Resolver Query Logging|Amazon VPC|AWS WAF|Internet Gateway
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Transit Gateway|AWS Network Firewall|NAT Gateway|Internet Gateway|Route 53 Resolver Query Logging
hint: A central hub must aggregate traffic from all 30 VPCs before it reaches the inspection point.
hint2: Domain-based filtering requires a stateful firewall that can inspect TLS SNI headers and HTTP host headers.
explanation: AWS Transit Gateway aggregates outbound traffic from all 30 VPCs into a centralized egress VPC. AWS Network Firewall inspects the traffic using domain-based allow/deny rules by examining TLS SNI headers and HTTP host headers, blocking traffic to unapproved domains. NAT Gateway translates private IPs to public, and the Internet Gateway provides the path to the internet. Route 53 Resolver Query Logging captures all DNS queries across all VPCs for the security audit trail.

scenarioDescription: A startup needs to deploy a containerized application across two Availability Zones with automatic scaling. The containers need to pull images from Amazon ECR without traversing the public internet, and the application must be accessible via HTTPS with a custom domain name and SSL termination.
toolboxServices: Application Load Balancer|Amazon ECS|AWS PrivateLink (VPC Endpoints)|Amazon ECR|Amazon Route 53|AWS Certificate Manager|NAT Gateway
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Route 53|AWS Certificate Manager|Application Load Balancer|Amazon ECS|AWS PrivateLink (VPC Endpoints)|Amazon ECR
hint: DNS resolution of the custom domain name is always the first step in any web request flow.
hint2: VPC interface endpoints allow ECS tasks to pull container images from ECR without using a NAT Gateway.
explanation: Amazon Route 53 resolves the custom domain to the ALB. AWS Certificate Manager provides the SSL/TLS certificate for HTTPS termination. The Application Load Balancer distributes traffic across ECS tasks in two AZs and terminates SSL. Amazon ECS runs the containerized application with auto-scaling. AWS PrivateLink via VPC interface endpoints allows ECS tasks in private subnets to pull images from Amazon ECR entirely over the AWS private network, eliminating the need for a NAT Gateway and avoiding public internet traversal.

scenarioDescription: A company acquired another business and needs to connect their two AWS environments. Both organizations unfortunately used the same CIDR range (10.0.0.0/16) for their VPCs. They need to enable communication between specific services in each VPC without re-addressing either network.
toolboxServices: AWS PrivateLink|VPC Peering|AWS Transit Gateway|Network Load Balancer|NAT Gateway|Application Load Balancer|Amazon VPC
correctPipelineOrder: Network Load Balancer|AWS PrivateLink
hint: VPC Peering cannot work when two VPCs have overlapping CIDR ranges.
hint2: This service creates private connectivity that works regardless of overlapping IP address spaces.
explanation: VPC Peering and Transit Gateway both fail when VPCs have overlapping CIDR ranges, eliminating those options. A Network Load Balancer is placed in front of the target service in one VPC, and AWS PrivateLink creates an endpoint service backed by that NLB. The consuming VPC creates an interface VPC endpoint that receives a private IP from its own CIDR range, enabling communication without any IP conflicts because PrivateLink abstracts away the underlying network addressing.

scenarioDescription: A media company needs to accelerate file transfers of 500GB video files from production studios in Tokyo and London to an S3 bucket in us-east-1. Standard internet uploads are too slow and unreliable, and they need to maximize throughput using the AWS backbone network.
toolboxServices: Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration|AWS Direct Connect|Amazon CloudFront|AWS Global Accelerator|AWS DataSync|Amazon S3|AWS Snow Family
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration|Amazon S3
hint: This S3 feature uses CloudFront edge locations to accelerate uploads over the AWS backbone network.
hint2: The studios upload over the public internet, but traffic enters the AWS network at the nearest edge location.
explanation: Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration is the correct choice because it leverages Amazon CloudFront's globally distributed edge locations to accelerate uploads. Video files from Tokyo and London enter the AWS backbone network at the nearest edge location, then travel over AWS's optimized private network to the S3 bucket in us-east-1, dramatically improving throughput and reliability compared to standard internet transfers. Direct Connect would require physical circuit provisioning at each studio location, making it impractical for this use case.

scenarioDescription: A company runs a latency-sensitive trading application that requires single-digit microsecond network latency between EC2 instances. The instances must be placed as physically close together as possible within an Availability Zone, and the application uses high-performance computing (HPC) communication patterns.
toolboxServices: EC2 Placement Groups (Cluster)|EC2 Placement Groups (Spread)|EC2 Placement Groups (Partition)|Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA)|Enhanced Networking (ENA)|AWS Nitro System|Amazon FSx for Lustre
correctPipelineOrder: EC2 Placement Groups (Cluster)|Enhanced Networking (ENA)|Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA)
hint: A specific placement strategy packs instances onto the same underlying hardware rack for minimum latency.
hint2: Beyond placement, a specialized network adapter bypasses the OS kernel for HPC inter-node communication.
explanation: EC2 Placement Groups (Cluster) places all instances on the same rack within a single AZ, minimizing physical network distance for single-digit microsecond latency. Enhanced Networking (ENA) provides high bandwidth (up to 100 Gbps) and reduced jitter using SR-IOV hardware virtualization. Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) enables OS-bypass communication for HPC workloads, allowing instances to communicate using libfabric with latencies comparable to on-premises HPC clusters.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to migrate 500 existing VPN connections from individual Virtual Private Gateways to a centralized hub. They want to use Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) routing to aggregate VPN bandwidth beyond the 1.25 Gbps limit of a single VPN tunnel, targeting 10 Gbps aggregate throughput.
toolboxServices: AWS Transit Gateway|Virtual Private Gateway|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|AWS Direct Connect|AWS Cloud WAN|AWS Network Manager|Amazon VPC
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Transit Gateway|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|AWS Network Manager
hint: Only one AWS service supports ECMP routing for VPN connections to aggregate bandwidth beyond single-tunnel limits.
hint2: After centralizing VPN connections, a management tool provides global visibility into the network topology.
explanation: AWS Transit Gateway is the only service that supports ECMP routing for Site-to-Site VPN connections, allowing multiple VPN tunnels to be aggregated for higher throughput — approximately 8 VPN connections with ECMP can achieve ~10 Gbps. AWS Site-to-Site VPN connections are terminated on the Transit Gateway instead of individual Virtual Private Gateways, centralizing all 500 connections. AWS Network Manager provides a global dashboard to visualize, monitor, and manage the entire network topology across Regions.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a zero-trust network architecture where every API call between microservices is authenticated and encrypted, regardless of whether the services are in the same VPC. They need mutual TLS (mTLS) enforcement and fine-grained authorization policies at the service level.
toolboxServices: Amazon VPC Lattice|AWS App Mesh|AWS PrivateLink|AWS WAF|Security Groups|Network ACLs|AWS Certificate Manager
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon VPC Lattice|AWS Certificate Manager
hint: AWS released a service specifically designed for service-to-service connectivity with built-in authentication and authorization.
hint2: This service supports auth policies that can enforce IAM-based access control at the individual service level.
explanation: Amazon VPC Lattice is purpose-built for service-to-service networking with zero-trust principles — it provides built-in mutual TLS for encryption, IAM-based auth policies for fine-grained authorization, and works across VPCs and accounts without requiring peering or Transit Gateway. AWS Certificate Manager provides and manages the TLS certificates used by VPC Lattice for mTLS enforcement. VPC Lattice is preferred over App Mesh here because it natively handles authentication and authorization without requiring sidecar proxy management.

scenarioDescription: A company hosts a multi-region active-active application and needs DNS failover that can detect application-level health issues, not just TCP connectivity. If the application in us-east-1 returns HTTP 500 errors, traffic must automatically shift to eu-west-1 within 30 seconds.
toolboxServices: Amazon Route 53|Route 53 Health Checks|Application Load Balancer|AWS Global Accelerator|Amazon CloudWatch|AWS Lambda|Network Load Balancer
correctPipelineOrder: Route 53 Health Checks|Amazon Route 53|Application Load Balancer
hint: Application-level health checking requires HTTP/HTTPS health checks that validate response status codes, not just port availability.
hint2: The DNS service uses health check results to automatically remove unhealthy endpoints from DNS responses.
explanation: Route 53 Health Checks are configured with HTTP/HTTPS checks that monitor the application endpoint and validate that it returns a 2xx or 3xx status code — detecting HTTP 500 errors as unhealthy. Amazon Route 53 uses failover routing policy associated with these health checks to automatically stop returning DNS records for the unhealthy us-east-1 endpoint and direct all traffic to eu-west-1. The Application Load Balancer in each Region distributes traffic to the application instances, and the fast health check interval (10 seconds) with a low failure threshold enables failover within 30 seconds.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to connect 15 branch offices to AWS and to each other through a managed global WAN. Each branch has an SD-WAN appliance, and the company wants centralized policy management, automated route propagation, and the ability to segment traffic between business units across all locations.
toolboxServices: AWS Cloud WAN|AWS Transit Gateway|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|AWS Network Manager|AWS Direct Connect|VPC Peering|Amazon VPC
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Cloud WAN|AWS Site-to-Site VPN|AWS Network Manager
hint: AWS offers a managed global WAN service that automates network creation, management, and monitoring across Regions.
hint2: Branch offices connect to the global WAN using IPsec tunnels from their existing SD-WAN appliances.
explanation: AWS Cloud WAN provides the managed global WAN backbone with centralized policy management, automated route propagation, and network segmentation using segments to isolate business unit traffic. AWS Site-to-Site VPN connects the 15 branch office SD-WAN appliances to Cloud WAN core network attachment points via IPsec tunnels. AWS Network Manager provides the centralized dashboard for monitoring the entire global WAN topology, connection health, and traffic patterns across all branches and AWS Regions.
